texts.shortText: Low, succulent, salt-tolerant annual bush with a short taproot and fleshy branching stems that are regularly jointed. Leaves not obvious and reduced to small fleshy lobes. Small flowers arranged in threes at joints in stem – the central flower the largest. Usually green but turns red when older or salt stressed.

Global: Endemic to Southern Africa.

Local/Regional: Southern Namibia to KwaZulu-Natal.

Salt-tolerant plant that occurs low on soft substrata both on the margins of marine lagoons and in estuaries, close to the high water mark. Also found in saline environments inland. May consist of more than one regional subspecies.

Species in this genus have a long history of use as food plants in Europe and North America. The stem segments can be eaten raw in salads, pickled, or cooked as a vegetable.
Members of this genus are typically wind pollinated and produce small seeds which are dispersed by wind or water.

This genus contains several other local species. The Coastal samphire S. littorea forms similar or larger, woody bushes, but is a perennial and is found above the high water mark on rocky shores, rather than on soft substrate. Several shorter species such as the Glasswort samphire S. tegetaria form creeping underground runners and send up numerous short vertical stems to create a ‘lawn’ in the upper intertidal of estuaries and lagoons.

Branch, G, Griffiths, CL, Branch, M & Beckley, L (2016). Two Oceans: A Guide to the Marine Life of Southern Africa (Fourth edition). Struik Nature, Cape Town. (p. 426).

Ozturk, M, Altay, V, Orçen, N, Yaprak, AE, Tuğ, GN & Güvensen, A (2018). A little-known and a little-consumed natural resource: Salicornia. In: Global perspectives on underutilized crops (pp. 83-108). Cham: Springer International Publishing.

Slenzka, A, Mucina, L & Kadereit, G (2013). Salicornia L.(Amaranthaceae) in South Africa and Namibia: rapid spread and ecological diversification of cryptic species. Botanical Journal of the Linnean Society, 172(2), 175-186.

Steffen, S, Mucina, L & Kadereit, G (2010). Revision of Sarcocornia (Chenopodiaceae) in South Africa, Namibia and Mozambique. Systematic Botany, 35, 390-408.
